definition: Interest calculated on both the initial principal and the accumulated interest from previous periods. It's how your money grows exponentially over time.
The real rate of return on a savings deposit, accounting for the effect of compounding interest. Higher APY means more earnings.
Money set aside for unexpected expenses — like medical bills, car repairs, or job loss. Ideally 3-6 months of living expenses.
A plan for how you'll allocate your income. It helps you track money coming in and going out so you can meet financial goals.
A simple budgeting framework: 50% of income for needs, 30% for wants, and 20% for savings and debt repayment.
A type of mutual fund that tracks a market index (like the S&P 500). Offers broad diversification at low cost — great for beginners.
Similar to an index fund but trades like a stock on an exchange. Can be bought and sold throughout the trading day.
Spreading your investments across different assets to reduce risk. 'Don't put all your eggs in one basket.'
The gradual increase in prices over time, which reduces the purchasing power of money. Average historical rate is around 2-3% per year.
Everything you own (assets) minus everything you owe (debts). A key measure of financial health.
Your total monthly debt payments divided by your gross monthly income. Lenders use this to assess your ability to manage payments.
An employer-sponsored retirement savings plan. Contributions are often tax-deferred and some employers match contributions — essentially free money.
A retirement account where you contribute after-tax money, but withdrawals in retirement are tax-free. Great for younger investors.
Investing a fixed amount regularly regardless of market conditions. This reduces the impact of volatility over time.
How easily an asset can be converted to cash. Savings accounts are highly liquid; real estate is not.
The profit from selling an investment for more than you paid. Can be short-term (taxed higher) or long-term (taxed lower).
A number (300-850) that represents your creditworthiness. Higher scores get better loan terms and interest rates.
The potential benefit you miss when choosing one option over another. Every financial decision has an opportunity cost.
Money set aside over time for a specific planned expense, like a vacation or new laptop. Prevents debt for expected costs.
A savings account offering significantly higher interest rates than traditional savings, often 10-20x more. Usually found at online banks.
Expenses that stay the same each month, like rent, car payments, or subscriptions. Easy to plan around.
Expenses that change month to month, like groceries, gas, or entertainment. Harder to predict but most flexible to cut.
How your investment portfolio is divided among different asset classes (stocks, bonds, cash). Should match your risk tolerance and goals.
A market decline of 20% or more from recent highs. Can be unsettling but is a normal part of market cycles.
A period of rising stock prices, generally by 20% or more. Bull markets tend to last longer than bear markets.
A quick way to estimate how long it takes to double your money: divide 72 by the annual return rate. At 8%, money doubles in ~9 years.
A method where every dollar of income is assigned a purpose (expenses, savings, investing), so income minus outgoing equals zero.
Income earned with minimal active effort, like dividends from investments, rental income, or royalties.
Price-to-Earnings ratio — a stock's current price divided by earnings per share. Helps assess if a stock is over- or under-valued.
The process of paying off a loan through regular payments over time. Early payments go more toward interest; later ones toward principal.
